name: read_file
content:
1	# taskq — a durable in-process task queue
     2	
     3	We need a small Python library, `taskq`, that lets a single application enqueue
     4	background work and process it reliably, surviving a crash of the host process.
     5	It is deliberately not a distributed system: one machine, one SQLite file, one
     6	or more worker threads inside the same process.
     7	
     8	## What it must do
     9	
    10	Callers enqueue a named task with a JSON-serializable payload. Workers pull
    11	tasks and run a registered handler function for that name. A task that raises is
    12	retried later; a task that keeps failing eventually stops being retried and is
    13	set aside for a human to inspect. Nothing may be lost if the process is killed
    14	mid-run, and no task may be executed by two workers at once.
    15	
    16	Callers also need to enqueue work for the future — "run this in ten minutes" —
    17	and to avoid duplicate work when the same logical job is enqueued twice by
    18	racing callers.
    19	
    20	## Constraints
    21	
    22	- Python 3.10+, standard library only. SQLite via the built-in sqlite3 module.
    23	- Multiple worker threads in one process must be safe. Assume the caller may
    24	  also enqueue from several threads.
    25	- A worker that is killed (SIGKILL, power loss) must not leave a task stuck
    26	  claimed forever — the task must become runnable again.
    27	- Retries must not hammer a failing dependency: successive attempts back off.
    28	- Throughput target is modest — hundreds of tasks per second, not millions.
    29	- Observability matters: an operator must be able to ask how many tasks are
    30	  pending, running, failed, and dead.
    31	
    32	## Explicitly out of scope
    33	
    34	Cross-machine coordination, priority classes, task cancellation, scheduled cron
    35	expressions, and a web UI. Do not design these.
    36	
    37	## Open questions the design must answer
    38	
    39	- How is a task cl…<clipped 331 chars>
